  - `reason` (optional) — free-text reason ("duplicate", "fraudulent",
    etc.).
- `GET /v1/refunds/{id}` — retrieve.

**Cascade on refund success.**
1. `Charge.amount_refunded += refund.amount` (held under the same lock as
   the validation so concurrent refunds can't double-spend).
2. `Charge.refunded = true` if amount_refunded reaches charge.amount.
3. If `reverse_transfer && charge.transfer != ""`: increment
   `Transfer.amount_reversed` by min(refund.amount, remaining).
4. Refund stored. Async webhook fanout: `charge.refunded` with the
   *updated* Charge as the event payload.

**What's not modelled.** Async-pending refunds (ACH, etc. — real Stripe
returns status=`pending` and resolves later via `refund.updated`).
Refund failures (no failure simulation on the create endpoint yet).
ApplicationFeeRefund as a distinct resource. TransferReversal as a
distinct retrievable resource (only the ID is surfaced on the Refund).

**API surface (Payouts — Connect-aware).**
- `POST /v1/payouts` — create a manually-triggered payout. Reads the
  `Stripe-Account` request header to scope the payout to a connected
  account; without the header, the payout is on the platform's own
  balance. Stored in `pending` status with `automatic=false`.
  Required: `amount` (positive int), `currency`. Optional: `method`
  (standard|instant, default standard), `source_type` (default
  bank_account), `description`, `statement_descriptor`, `metadata`.
  `arrival_date` derived from method (now for instant, +48h for standard).
  Validates: connected account exists if Stripe-Account header set
  (mirrors real Stripe's 404 on typos).
- `GET /v1/payouts/{id}` — retrieve.
- `GET /v1/payouts` — list, scoped by `Stripe-Account` header. Honors
  `?limit=` (1-100, default 10). Sorted newest-first. Cursor pagination
  (`starting_after`) accepted but not honored — overkill for a dev mock.
  This is the canonical marketplace dashboard query: "show all payouts to
  this seller's connected account".

**Dev UI (Payout).**
- `GET /__hamr/stripe/payout?id=<po_id>` — renders amount, expected
  arrival date, connected account tag (if any), method, and Mark
  paid / Mark failed buttons. Already-terminal payouts return 410.
- `POST /__hamr/stripe/payout/complete` — outcomes: `paid` or `fail`.

**Outcome cascades.**
| Button | Event | State after |
|---|---|---|
| Mark paid | `payout.paid` | status=`paid` |
| Mark failed | `payout.failed` | status=`failed`, failure_code=`account_closed`, failure_message=`The bank account has been closed.` |

(Failure code/message are hardcoded today; if you need to test other
codes, the rule is in the `payoutOutcomes` map in
`stripemock_payout_page.go` — extend with more outcomes or wire a form
field through `handlePayoutComplete`.)

**What's not modelled.** Automatic payouts (real Stripe creates payouts
on a schedule from the connected account's balance). Payout cancel /
reverse endpoints. The `failed → paid` recovery path that some banks
trigger after an initial failure. ReconciliationStatus, BalanceTransaction.

**Validation.**
- All line items in a session must share the same currency. Mock surfaces
  this as a 400 with a typed `*stripe.Error` shape so `stripe-go` callers
  receive it as `&stripe.Error{...}`.
- Unknown sessions on retrieve return a 404 in Stripe's canonical error shape.

**Webhook delivery.** When the user clicks an outcome on the dev UI, the
mock fires a real signed webhook (HMAC-SHA256 over `<unix>.<payload>`,
header `Stripe-Signature: t=<unix>,v1=<hex>`) to the configured
`webhook_url` with `webhook_secret`. The app's existing
`webhook.ConstructEvent(body, sig, secret)` verifies it as production. The
event envelope includes `api_version` (set to the mock's pinned version)
which clears stripe-go's release-train compatibility check.

**Outcome → event mapping (checkout).**
| Button | Event(s) | Status, payment_status | Redirect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pay Successfully | `checkout.session.completed`, `payment_intent.succeeded`, `charge.succeeded` (PI + Charge materialised) | complete, paid | success_url |
| Card Declined | *(none — synchronous decline)* | stays open | back to checkout page |
| Cancel Payment | `checkout.session.expired` | expired, unpaid | cancel_url |

**Outcome → event mapping (account onboarding).**
| Action | Event | State after |
|---|---|---|
| Complete Onboarding | `account.updated` | charges_enabled=true, payouts_enabled=true, details_submitted=true, currently_due=[] |

**Outcome → event mapping (PaymentIntent).**
| Button | Cascade (in order) | PI status after |
|---|---|---|
| Succeed (destination charge) | `payment_intent.succeeded`, `charge.succeeded`, `transfer.created` | succeeded |
| Succeed (no destination) | `payment_intent.succeeded`, `charge.succeeded` | succeeded |
| Fail | `payment_intent.payment_failed` | requires_payment_method |

**Outcome → event mapping (Refund).** No dev UI button — refunds are
created by the app via `refund.New()`. The mock applies state changes
synchronously and fires one webhook async.
| Action | Event | State after |
|---|---|---|
| `refund.New(payment_intent=...)` (full) | `charge.refunded` | refund.status=succeeded, charge.refunded=true |
| `refund.New(charge=..., amount=N)` (partial) | `charge.refunded` | refund.status=succeeded, charge.amount_refunded += N |
| `refund.New(... reverse_transfer=true)` | `charge.refunded` | + transfer.amount_reversed += refund.amount |

**Outcome → event mapping (Payout).**
| Button | Event | State after |
|---|---|---|
| Mark paid | `payout.paid` | status=`paid` |
| Mark failed | `payout.failed` | status=`failed`, failure_code+failure_message populated |

**Race-safety.** Once a session leaves "open", subsequent outcome POSTs
return 409 Conflict so a double-click can only fire one webhook. The
checkout page also returns 410 Gone for already-completed sessions to
prevent re-use from a stale browser tab.

**API version pinning.** Constant `stripeAPIVersion` in
`internal/devserver/stripemock.go` must equal `stripe.APIVersion` from the
SDK in `go.mod`. A CI test (`TestStripeAPIVersionMatchesSDK`) enforces the
link, so a `stripe-go` bump fails the build until the constant is bumped in
lockstep. Current pinned value: `2025-08-27.basil` (matches `stripe-go/v82`).

**CSRF guard.** Outcome-recording POST rejects requests whose `Origin`
header is set and doesn't match the request's `Host`. Requests without an
`Origin` (curl, tests) pass through.

**Config (`hamr.toml`).**
```toml
[dev.stripe]
enabled        = true
webhook_url    = "http://localhost:8080/api/webhooks/stripe"   # required
webhook_secret = "whsec_dev_..."                               # required
persist        = true                                          # default
persist_path   = ".hamr/stripe/state.json"                     # default
```
`enabled = true` requires both `webhook_url` and `webhook_secret`, plus a
`[proxy]` section (the API and UI both live on the proxy mux); `hamr dev`
refuses to start otherwise with a typed error.

**Persistence.** Single JSON file at `persist_path`, atomically rewritten
(tmp + rename) on every state mutation under the same lock that gates the
mutation. Loaded on `NewStripeMock` if the path is set; corrupt files
trigger the `OnPersistError` callback and the mock starts empty (no
crash). Schema is forward-compatible — `encoding/json` ignores unknown
fields and zeroes missing ones, so adding fields is safe. Set
`persist = false` for in-memory-only mode.

Why JSON not mbox: Stripe state is plain Go structs (no MIME equivalent),
so a single JSON document is the natural shape. The file is human-readable
which helps LLM workflows that want to read prior state across restarts.

**Dashboard.** `GET /__hamr/stripe` renders an index with 5 tables —
sessions, accounts, PaymentIntents, refunds, payouts — newest-first,
capped at 25 rows per table. Designed both for human dev use and for
LLMs reading the page to inspect prior state.

Per-row actions (all POST endpoints, same-origin guarded):
- `POST /__hamr/stripe/resend` (`resource=`, `id=`) — re-fires the
  natural event(s) for the resource's current state. PI succeed re-fires
  the full 3-event cascade in order; refund re-fires `charge.refunded`
  with the post-refund Charge state; payout re-fires the matching
  `payout.paid` or `payout.failed`. Returns 400 if there's no natural
  event for the current state (e.g., open session).
- `POST /__hamr/stripe/refund` (`payment_intent=`, `amount=`,
  `reverse_transfer`, `refund_application_fee`) — issues a refund directly
  from the dashboard. Routes through the same `applyRefund` path
  `refund.New` uses, so validation + Charge mutation + Transfer reversal
  all behave identically.
- `POST /__hamr/stripe/expire` (`session=`) — marks an open session as
  expired and fires `checkout.session.expired`. 409 on already-terminal.

**Dev panel button.** `sseConfig.StripeMock` (JSON key `stripe_mock`) is
set when `[dev.stripe].enabled = true`. The dev-panel JS in `reload.js`
renders an "Open Stripe mock" shortcut alongside the existing "Open mail
inbox" button when this flag is present.

**Scaffold integration.** `hamr new --stripe` generates a single random
`whsec_dev_<32 hex>` at scaffold time and writes it to both `.env`
(`STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET=...`) and `hamr.toml` (`[dev.stripe].webhook_secret`)
so the two stay in sync. Generated `cmd/site/main.go` reads `STRIPE_MOCK`
and conditionally calls `stripe.SetBackend(...)`:
```go
stripe.Key = envStripeKey
if envStripeMock {
    if !envDevMode {
        log.Error("STRIPE_MOCK=true requires DEV_MODE=true (refusing to route Stripe calls to a local mock without dev mode)")
        os.Exit(1)
    }
    stripe.SetBackend(stripe.APIBackend, stripe.GetBackendWithConfig(
        stripe.APIBackend,
        &stripe.BackendConfig{URL: stripe.String(envHamrStripeMockURL)},
    ))
}
```
`.env.example` ships with `STRIPE_MOCK=true` and a non-empty `STRIPE_KEY`.
The mock URL is auto-injected by `hamr dev` as `HAMR_STRIPE_MOCK_URL`,
derived from `hamr.toml` `[proxy].listen` — no hardcoded port to keep in
sync. Production deployments leave `STRIPE_MOCK` unset so `stripe-go`
reaches `api.stripe.com`, and `[dev.stripe]` is absent from production
`hamr.toml`.

**Production safety guard.** `STRIPE_MOCK=true && DEV_MODE=false` refuses
to start. A leftover `STRIPE_MOCK=true` in a production `.env` would
otherwise route real payment calls to a non-existent localhost endpoint
and silently break checkouts. Staging that wants fake payments should
also set `DEV_MODE=true`.

**Hamr-injected env vars.** When `hamr dev` spawns the site process it
sets two env vars derived from `hamr.toml` config:
- `HAMR_DEV_URL=http://localhost:<proxy_port>` when either `[dev.email]`
  or `[dev.stripe]` is enabled. The scaffold's email mock client uses it.
- `HAMR_STRIPE_MOCK_URL=http://localhost:<proxy_port>` when
  `[dev.stripe].enabled = true`. Scaffolded `main.go` points stripe-go
  at this URL when `STRIPE_MOCK=true`.

These pre-set vars win over `.env` (godotenv.Load doesn't overwrite
already-set env), so users can't accidentally drift the mock URL from
the actual proxy port. Hamr knows its own URL — nothing in `.env` should
be more authoritative than that.

**Known limits.** Mocked today: checkout sessions, Connect account
onboarding (`Account` + `AccountLink`), Connect-aware PaymentIntents
(with auto-cascade to Charge + Transfer on success), Refunds
(sync-success with `reverse_transfer` and `refund_application_fee`),
and Payouts (manual create + paid/failed outcomes, scoped list for
marketplace dashboards). Not yet mocked: standalone Transfer create,
Customer, Price, Subscription, Invoice, Dispute, GET endpoints for
Charge / Transfer / ApplicationFee. Patterns are established in
`internal/devserver/stripemock*.go` — add as needed. State is persisted
by default to `.hamr/stripe/state.json` and reloaded across `hamr dev`
restarts; set `persist = false` for ephemeral in-memory-only mode.

**Logging.** Mock log lines are emitted via a dedicated logger tagged
`[hamr:stripe]` (the standard `[hamr dev]` lines come from the proxy and
file-watcher). Plumbed via a `component=stripe` slog attribute that
hamr's `devHandler` interprets and strips before formatting, so attrs in
the log line itself are unaffected. Most common emitted lines:
`webhook delivery failed` (app server down or non-2xx response),
`dashboard resend failed`, `dashboard refund webhook delivery failed`,
`dashboard expire webhook delivery failed`, and a one-shot `mock enabled`
at boot with the proxy URL the mock is reachable on.

---

### email

> Package email defines a provider-agnostic `Sender` interface for
> transactional email and the value types that flow through it. Apps depend on
> the interface; concrete implementations (real provider adapters, emailmock,
> plain SMTP) are chosen at startup.

```go
type Sender interface {
    Send(ctx context.Context, msg Message) (*Result, error)
}
```
Implementations must be safe for concurrent use. `Send` respects context
cancellation.

```go
type Message struct {
    From        Address
    To, Cc, Bcc []Address
    ReplyTo     *Address          // nil if not overriding From
    Subject     string
    Text        string            // plaintext body
    HTML        string            // html body
    Attachments []Attachment      // downloadable files
    Inline      []Attachment      // cid-referenced; ContentID must be set
    Headers     map[string]string // custom SMTP/HTTP headers
    Tags        map[string]string // free-form correlation metadata
}

type Address    struct { Name, Email string }
type Attachment struct { Filename, ContentType string; Data []byte; ContentID string }
type Result     struct { ID string } // provider-assigned message id (may be empty)

func Addr(name, email string) Address
```

**Body semantics.** Set `Text`, `HTML`, or both. Real adapters producing MIME
emit `multipart/alternative` when both are present.

**Inline images.** Reference as `<img src="cid:logo">` in HTML; add an
Attachment to `Inline` with `ContentID = "logo"`. The emailmock viewer rewrites
`cid:` to local URLs; real adapters include the parts in the MIME tree.

**Extension points.** The interface is deliberately narrow. Provider-specific
features belong in:
- `Headers` — custom SMTP/RFC5322 headers (List-Unsubscribe, X-*, In-Reply-To)
- `Tags` — correlation metadata (user_id, campaign_id) for provider webhooks
- Your own adapter — templates, per-recipient substitutions, scheduled send,
  tracking flags

**Swap pattern.**
```go
var sender email.Sender
if os.Getenv("EMAIL_MOCK") == "true" {
    sender = emailmock.New(os.Getenv("HAMR_DEV_URL"))
} else {
    sender = myapp.NewRealSender(cfg)
}
```

---

### emailmock

> Package emailmock is a dev-only `email.Sender` that ships messages to the
> hamr dev server's in-memory inbox at `/__hamr/mail`. It does not construct
> MIME, talk SMTP, or call any provider API — it POSTs JSON to the hamr dev
> proxy's ingest endpoint.

**What this mock tests.** How your application uses `email.Sender` — field
population, recipient handling, attachment construction, HTML/text body
rendering, inline image CIDs. It does NOT validate your production provider
adapter's MIME construction, SMTP handshake, or HTTP wire format. Use your
provider's test environment or integration tests for those.

```go
func New(baseURL string) *Client
```
`baseURL` is the hamr dev proxy origin (e.g. `http://localhost:3000`).
Trailing slashes are stripped. Empty baseURL is accepted but every `Send` will
fail until reconfigured. Default request timeout is 10 seconds.

```go
func (c *Client) Send(ctx context.Context, msg email.Message) (*email.Result, error)
```
JSON-encodes `msg` and POSTs to `<baseURL>/__hamr/mail/ingest`. On 200 returns
the server-assigned `*email.Result{ID}`; on 422 with a magic-recipient body
returns `ErrBounced` or `ErrRejected`; on 413 returns a size-limit error;
otherwise returns a wrapped HTTP error.

```go
var (
    ErrBounced  = errors.New("emailmock: recipient bounced")
    ErrRejected = errors.New("emailmock: recipient rejected")
)
```
Sentinel errors; use `errors.Is` to detect.

**Failure simulation.** Any recipient whose local-part (case-insensitive)
matches `bounce` or `reject` short-circuits storage:

| Local-part | Returned error |
|------------|----------------|
| `bounce`   | `ErrBounced`   |
| `reject`   | `ErrRejected`  |

Checked across `To`, `Cc`, and `Bcc`. Nothing is stored in the inbox for
rejected messages.

**Dev inbox UI** (served at `/__hamr/mail` on the hamr dev proxy):

- Inbox list — newest-first, substring search over subject, recipients, sender
- Detail view — tabs: HTML / Plaintext / Raw / Headers / Attachments
- HTML tab — sandboxed `<iframe sandbox="allow-popups allow-popups-to-escape-sandbox">`
  (no JS, no forms, no top-nav, no same-origin) with restrictive CSP
  (`default-src 'none'; img-src 'self' data:; style-src 'unsafe-inline'`),
  mobile-viewport toggle (375px), images-blocked toggle. Link clicks open
  in a new tab — every `<a>` is rewritten so its `target` is `_blank`, plus
  `<base target="_blank">` in the iframe document as a fallback.
- Raw tab — best-effort RFC822-shaped reconstruction (NOT real MIME; the
  mock never receives MIME)
- Outcome simulation — per-message Mark Failed / Mark Delayed for post-hoc
  status tagging on already-captured messages
- Inbox-wide Clear, per-message Delete

**HTTP ingest API** (for non-Go clients):

```
POST /__hamr/mail/ingest
Content-Type: application/json

{"From":{"Email":"..."}, "To":[{"Email":"..."}], "Subject":"...", "HTML":"...", ...}

→ 200  {"ID":"msg_..."}
→ 413  {"error":"message too large"}
→ 422  {"error":"bounced"}  or  {"error":"rejected"}
```
Attachment `Data` is base64-encoded per Go's JSON conventions.

**Server-side config** (in `hamr.toml`):
```toml
[dev.email]
enabled           = true                     # opt-in; default false
max_messages      = 500                      # ring-buffer size; oldest evicted first
max_message_bytes = 10485760                 # per-message cap; ingest returns 413 above this
persist           = true                     # default; mirror inbox to mbox file
persist_path      = ".hamr/mail/inbox.mbox"  # default
```
Requires `[proxy]` to be configured — inbox UI lives on the proxy mux.
`hamr dev` refuses to start if `enabled = true` without a proxy.

**Lifecycle.** Inbox survives app restarts triggered by file-watch rebuilds
(the dev proxy keeps running). When persistence is enabled (default) it
also survives restarts of `hamr dev` itself via the mbox file.

**Persistence format.** Standard MBOXO mbox. Each message is a real RFC 822 /
MIME document separated by a `From ` line, so Thunderbird, mutt, and Apple
Mail can open the file directly. Runtime metadata (status, tags, received-at,
internal id) round-trips via `X-Hamr-*` headers that real mail clients
ignore. Bodies not ending in `\n` gain one trailing newline after round-trip
(MBOXO separator requirement). Set `persist = false` to skip the file.

**CSRF guard.** Mutating POST endpoints (`/clear`, `/:id/delete`,
`/:id/fail`, `/:id/delay`) reject requests whose `Origin` header is set but
doesn't match the request Host. Requests without an `Origin` header (curl,
tests, non-browser clients) pass through. The mock is unauthenticated — do
not bind `hamr dev`'s proxy to a public interface.

**Dev-only.** No production guards. Gate on `EMAIL_MOCK=true` in `main.go`.

**Known limits.**
- In-memory; no persistence across `hamr dev` restart
- Raw view is reconstructed, not true MIME wire bytes
- Single shared inbox — no per-user segmentation
- No webhook simulation, no open/click tracking simulation

---

## CLI Commands

### Version gating

Two commands refuse to run on version mismatch (released CLI builds only):

- **hamr new** — queries GitHub's latest release; if the local CLI is older,
  refuses to scaffold so generated projects never lag the template. Network
  errors only warn and fall through (offline/CI-friendly).
- **hamr dev** — if `[hamr] version` in `hamr.toml` is newer than the CLI,
  refuses to start. The opposite direction (CLI newer than scaffold) only
  warns and the status bar shows the mismatch.

Both accept `--skip-version-check` to bypass. Dev builds of the CLI (no
release tag) skip both checks entirely.

### `[dev].port_walk` and `hamr env`

`hamr dev` walks +1 on busy for every port it manages — the proxy listen,
the spawned-app `PORT`, and each `[[dev.docker_compose]]` service's host
ports — so two `hamr dev` instances on one machine (or a stray local
Postgres on 5432) don't collide. Default is on; set `[dev].port_walk =
false` to fail fast on EADDRINUSE for CI / pinned-port deployments. Each
shift logs a WARN.

When a port walks, values in `.env` referencing it (`DATABASE_URL`,
`S3_ENDPOINT`, etc.) get rewritten transparently:

- **In-process** (spawned site daemon, `hamr sync` daemon, custom
  daemons): hamr injects the rewritten KEY=VALUE into the child env on
  top of `.env`. Rule-level `Env` still wins on key conflict.
- **Out of process** (Makefile targets, shell scripts, standalone CLI
  invocations): `hamr dev` writes `.hamr/walks.json` after walking;
  `hamr env [--export]` reads it plus `.env` and emits the rewritten
  pairs. The scaffold's `Makefile` ships an `ENV_LOAD := eval
  "$(hamr env --export)"` prefix on `migrate`, `db-sh`, `generate`,
  `e2e-local`. `hamr sync` invoked standalone reads `walks.json`
  itself — no wrapping needed.

Match rules for which `.env` values get rewritten:

- `(localhost|127.0.0.1|0.0.0.0|[::1]):<port>` anywhere in the value →
  port swapped, host preserved.
- whole-value `:<port>` (Go listener form) → port swapped.

Values without an explicit port (e.g. `postgres://user@localhost/db`
relying on the scheme default) are not rewritten — include the port in
`.env` if you want auto-walking. Remote hosts (`db.prod.example.com:5432`)
are also left alone. Userinfo-shaped colons in URLs
(`postgres://user:5432pass@host:5432/db`) don't false-positive because
the rule requires the local-host prefix.

The whole-value `:<port>` rule rewrites connect and bind values
uniformly. Dependency-port URLs (consumers connecting to walked
publishers) work correctly. Bind-style values like `HEALTHCHECK_BIND=:8080`
or `METRICS_ADDR=:9090` get the same treatment without per-var
re-probing — the rewritten port could collide if multiple things in the
project bind the same number. Use distinct ports per binder.

`.hamr/walks.json` is regenerated on every `hamr dev` start and removed
when no walks are needed. It's gitignored under the existing `.hamr/`
rule. When `hamr dev` exits but the compose stack stays up
(`keep_running = true`), the file persists so `make migrate` invocations
that come later still resolve the right ports.

### Compose adoption path

On startup `hamr dev` queries each `[[dev.docker_compose]]` entry with
`docker compose ps --format json` before doing anything else. Failures
(daemon down, missing compose subcommand, parse errors) hard-fail
`hamr dev` with the underlying error — running with stale or unknown
state risks recreating containers the user wanted preserved.

Per entry, hamr decides between two paths:

- **Adopt** — every expected service (`dc.Services` if set; otherwise
  every service in the compose file) is `running` AND ready (`healthy`
  or no healthcheck defined). hamr skips `compose up -d` entirely and
  leaves the override file alone. Shifts for env injection come from
  diffing the `compose ps` published ports against the base compose
  file, so `DATABASE_URL` on the spawned app still points at whatever
  port a prior session walked the db to.
- **Apply** — at least one expected service is missing, exited,
  `unhealthy`, or `starting`. hamr walks only the non-running services
  (peers stay on whatever ports they're already bound to), passes the
  project's already-published ports as an "owned" set so the walk
  doesn't shift away from a port held by one of our own running
  containers, then runs `compose up -d`. The override file is rewritten
  to capture every running service's actual published port plus the
  walked ports for missing services. Running-unhealthy / starting peers
  fall in the "running" bucket too: their published port is preserved
  in the override so `compose up -d` doesn't see drift and recreate
  them — env injection still points consumers at where the container
  actually is. The user wipes the stack to force a recreate of an
  unhealthy peer.

Override management is state-aware: a stale `.hamr/compose.<name>.override.yaml`
is only auto-removed when the project has zero running containers AND
nothing drifted. Anything running → leave the override alone. This
keeps `keep_running` containers from bouncing on the next session.

Adoption applies regardless of `keep_running` — that flag governs
shutdown only. If a `keep_running = false` stack is somehow already up
at startup (manual `docker compose up`, prior crash recovery), hamr
adopts it just as it would a `keep_running = true` stack.

Adoption does not reconcile compose-file edits. If the user changes a
base port (or any other compose-file content) while a stack is still
up, the running containers keep their old config; hamr logs a `WARN`
per adopted-on-non-base-port service. To apply edits, wipe the stack
from the browser dev panel, run `docker compose down -v` directly, or
wire it into a Makefile target and trigger it via the TUI's `m`
fuzzy palette.

The file is the contract surface between the writer (hamr dev) and the
readers (`hamr env`, `hamr sync`, scaffold Makefile, scaffold
`db-shell.sh`). Schema is a flat `shifts` array of
`{kind, old, new, ...}` records — `kind` is one of `proxy`, `app`, or
`compose`; compose entries also carry `service` and `compose_name`.
Schema agreement between the two sides is pinned by the round-trip test
in `internal/devserver/walks_roundtrip_test.go`, which produces the
file via the writer and consumes it via the public resolver — schema
drift on either side breaks that test.

### hamr env

Print env-var rewrites derived from the running dev server's port walks.

```bash
hamr env                       # KEY=VALUE (cwd as project root)
hamr env --export              # export KEY=VALUE (single-quoted)
hamr env --dir "$ROOT"         # resolve walks/.env from $ROOT, not cwd
```

Reads `.hamr/walks.json` + `.env`, applies the same match rules `hamr dev`
uses internally, prints rewrites only for values that changed. When no
walks are recorded the command exits 0 with no output — sourcing it is a
no-op and the consumer falls through to `.env` unchanged.

Designed for embedding in Makefiles / shell scripts that run outside
`hamr dev` but need walked port values. The scaffold's `Makefile` does
this automatically.

### hamr dev (terminal hotkeys)

`hamr dev` runs as a bubbletea-driven TUI with a scrollable log viewport,
a status bar, modal overlays, and an `m` hotkey opening a fuzzy palette
over the project's `./Makefile` targets so docker wipes, migrations,
and any other project-defined scripts can be triggered from inside dev.

| Key                | Action                                                 |
|--------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| `r`                | Rebuild all watch rules in topological order           |
| `o`                | Open the proxy URL in the default browser              |
| `c`                | Clear the active tab's log buffer                      |
| `m`                | Run a Makefile target (fuzzy palette over `./Makefile`)|
| `Tab` / Shift+Tab  | Cycle log tabs (hamr ↔ docker stacks)                  |
| `/`                | Search active tab (case-insensitive substring, live)   |
| `n` / `N`          | Next / previous search match                           |
| `f`                | Toggle filter view (only matching lines)               |
| `Esc`              | Clear active selection (if any), else clear search     |
| `?`                | Toggle help overlay listing every binding              |
| `q` / Ctrl+C       | Quit gracefully                                        |
| ↑ / ↓              | Scroll log viewport line by line                       |
| PgUp / PgDn        | Scroll log viewport by page                            |
| mouse wheel        | Scroll log viewport                                    |
| click              | Select a log line (whole logical line, even wrapped)   |
| click + drag       | Extend selection across lines; auto-scrolls past edges |
| Shift+click        | Extend selection from anchor to clicked line           |
| Ctrl+click         | Toggle one line in/out of the selection                |
| `y`                | Copy selected lines to clipboard (raw text)            |

The runner also spawns one `docker compose --ansi=always -f <file> logs -f --tail=50` follower per `[[dev.docker_compose]]` entry. Output flows into a dedicated per-entry buffer that Tab cycles through; the status bar's left badge switches between 🔨 `hamr dev` and 🐳 `<stack-name>` to indicate which buffer the viewport is showing. Followers self-restart after a wipe so the stream resumes once `up -d` brings the project back. `--ansi=always` is the top-level compose flag that forces colour through despite the no-TTY pipe target, so per-service prefix colours and any container ANSI render correctly inside the bubbles viewport.

Search (`/`) is per-tab, case-insensitive substring, and incremental — matches recompute on every keystroke and the viewport jumps to the first hit as you type, with yellow highlights for matches and a brighter orange for the cursored one. `↩` locks the query in so `n`/`N` start navigating instead of being treated as more query characters; `Esc` cancels. After commit, `f` toggles a filter view that shows only lines containing the search term (press `f` again to restore the full view); n/N still navigates inside the filtered view. New log lines arriving while a search is in progress are scanned and the `[k/n]` counter updates automatically.

`m` opens a fuzzy palette listing every target in the project-root
`./Makefile` (parsed lazily on press; targets appear in declaration
order on empty query, ranked by `sahilm/fuzzy` as the user types).
The hotkey is hidden from the hint bar and ignored when no Makefile
exists. Selecting a target closes the palette, opens a small floating
"running" box, and runs `make <target>` from the project root with
stdout/stderr piped into the hamr tab line-prefixed `[make:<target>] `.
Only one target runs at a time. While running, every key is suppressed
except `q` (sends `SIGINT` to the make process) and `Ctrl+C` (cancels
the run and quits the TUI so children aren't orphaned). On exit the box
switches to a `Done ✓` / `Failed ✗ (exit N)` summary that stays until
the user dismisses it with any key. The earlier dedicated `d` docker
wipe hotkey was removed in favour of letting projects define their own
`docker-wipe`, `migrate`, etc. Makefile targets and chain whatever
recovery they need — the browser dev panel's `/__hamr/docker/{name}/wipe`
HTTP route still exists for projects that prefer that surface.

Long log lines soft-wrap to the viewport width (CSI escapes are passed through and don't count toward width); resizing the terminal re-wraps in place while preserving scroll-bottom-tracking. `q` and `Ctrl+C` quit cleanly even when the dev server is parked on a `hamr.toml` parse error — the config-error wait selects on the hotkey channel as well as fsnotify, so the user is never stuck on "waiting for config fix...".

Mouse-click selection: a left-press inside the viewport selects the whole logical line (reverse-video, including every wrap row of the same line). Holding the button and dragging extends the selection — and dragging past the top or bottom viewport edge auto-scrolls (one line per ~50 ms via tea.Tick) so the user can pick a range larger than the viewport. The auto-scroll loop stops once the cursor moves back inside or the viewport hits its scroll limit. Shift+click extends from the existing anchor; Ctrl+click toggles one line and disables drag-extend (ctrl is a single-line gesture, not a range). Selection is **global, not per-tab** — switching tabs drops it because the stored buffer indices wouldn't translate to the destination tab's buffer. While at least one line is selected, the bottom hint bar swaps to `[N selected]   y copy   esc clear` and log auto-scroll is paused so the picked lines aren't yanked off-screen by incoming output. `y` joins the selected lines (in original buffer order), strips ANSI escapes, and writes the result to the system clipboard via `atotto/clipboard` (`xclip`/`xsel`/`wl-copy` on Linux, `pbcopy` on macOS, `clip.exe` on Windows). On clipboard error the failure is appended as a `[hamr:tui] clipboard: <err>` line on the hamr tab so the user can see why instead of getting silence; otherwise the selection clears post-copy. The bounded log buffer eviction shifts selection indices in lockstep, dropping anything that scrolls off the start.

### hamr add skill

> Install an AI agent skill describing the HAMR framework.

Writes a self-contained skill directory the target AI coding tool can load. The skill covers the `hamr` CLI, the `github.com/FyrmForge/hamr/pkg/*` packages, and HAMR's Go + templ + HTMX coding practices (plus Alpine.js guidance when the project opted in via `hamr new --alpine`). The skill is rendered per project: project installs read `hamr.toml` to decide which sections to include; `--global` installs default Alpine off.

```text
hamr add skill <target> [flags]
```

Currently supported targets: `claude`. Additional targets (`codex`, `opencode`, etc.) will follow.

Flags:
- `--global` — install to `~/.<target>/skills/hamr/` instead of `./.<target>/skills/hamr/` (default: `false`)
- `--force` — overwrite an existing skill directory (default: `false`)

Destination resolution:
- Default: `./.claude/skills/hamr/`, and the command **must** be run from a HAMR project root (directory containing `hamr.toml`).
- `--global`: `~/.claude/skills/hamr/`, runnable from any directory.

Examples:
```bash
hamr add skill claude
hamr add skill claude --global
hamr add skill claude --force
```

The installed skill is a directory with `SKILL.md` plus three progressive-disclosure references under `references/` (CLI, packages, practices). The AI tool loads `SKILL.md` by default and pulls in a reference only when relevant to the current task.

### hamr ai capture

> Browser screenshot capture with HTML/text/metadata sidecars.

Launches a browser, navigates to the target URL, and saves a PNG screenshot.
Optional sidecars: visible page text (.txt), page HTML (.html), JSON metadata.

```text
hamr ai capture <url> [flags]
```

Flags:
- `--out <path>` — output PNG path; if omitted a per-capture folder is created
- `--dir <path>` — root directory for per-capture folders (default: `.hamr/ai/captures`)
- `--browser <path>` — browser binary path (auto-detects Chromium/Chrome/Brave)
- `--text` — save visible page text alongside the screenshot
- `--html` — save page HTML alongside the screenshot
- `--json` — print capture metadata as JSON
- `--selector <css>` — capture only the first element matching a CSS selector
- `--full-page` — capture the full scrollable page as one image
- `--tiles` — capture viewport-sized tiles from top to bottom (mutually exclusive with `--full-page` and `--selector`)
- `--tile-overlap <px>` — pixels of overlap between tiles (default: 120 when `--tiles` is set)
- `--width <px>` — viewport width in pixels (default: 1440)
- `--height <px>` — viewport height in pixels (default: 1024)
- `--scale <factor>` — device scale factor (default: 1)
- `--scroll-to <preset>` — scroll before capture: top, middle, or bottom
- `--scroll-x <px>` — horizontal scroll offset before capture
- `--scroll-y <px>` — vertical scroll offset before capture
- `--scroll-selector <css>` — CSS selector for a scroll container
- `--headless` — run browser in headless mode (default: true)
- `--no-sandbox` — launch with --no-sandbox (default: true)
- `--timeout <duration>` — timeout for page operations (default: 15s)
- `--wait <duration>` — extra delay after page load (default: 1s)

Capture modes:
- **viewport** (default) — captures exactly what the browser shows
- **full-page** (`--full-page`) — captures the entire scrollable page as one image
- **tiled** (`--tiles`) — captures viewport-sized screenshots top-to-bottom with configurable overlap; produces multiple PNGs (tile_001.png, tile_002.png, ...) ideal for LLM consumption of long pages

Tile output: when `--tiles` is used, individual tile PNGs are written to the
bundle directory. The meta.json includes `tile_paths`, `tile_count`,
`tile_overlap`, and `total_height` fields.

### hamr ai upgrade

> Git-based scaffold upgrade report comparing the project's baseline
> version to the current HAMR version.

Reads `[hamr]` and `[options]` sections from `hamr.toml` to determine the
project's scaffold baseline and options. Clones the HAMR repo (bare, partial
clone) and diffs between version tags to produce a unified diff and stat summary.

```text
hamr ai upgrade [flags]
```

Flags:
- `--json` — output as structured JSON
- `--from <version>` — override the base version to diff from (instead of reading hamr.toml)
- `--applied` — update `[hamr] version` in hamr.toml to the current HAMR version
- `--dir <path>` — directory to save the upgrade report (defaults to `.hamr/ai/upgrades/`)

Reports are automatically saved to `.hamr/ai/upgrades/` as JSON files with the
naming pattern `upgrade_{base}_to_{current}_{timestamp}.json`.

#### hamr.toml metadata

Scaffolded projects include metadata sections:

```toml
[hamr]
version = "0.3.2"
scaffolded_at = "2026-03-11"

[options]
database = "postgres"
db_connector = "sqlx"
auth = "session"
css = "tailwind"
websockets = false
e2e = false
stripe = false
locale = false
storage = "local"
alpine = false

[ai]
# dir = ".hamr/ai"
```

#### `[ai]` configuration

The `[ai]` section configures the AI command playground directory:

- `dir` — base directory for AI artifacts (default: `.hamr/ai`). Captures are
  written to `{dir}/captures/`, upgrade reports to `{dir}/upgrades/`.

#### JSON output shape

```json
{
  "project": {
    "base_version": "0.3.2",
    "current_version": "0.5.0",
    "scaffolded_at": "2026-03-11",
    "options": { ... }
  },
  "changes": [
    {
      "category": "structural",
      "title": "Change title",
      "since": "0.4.0",
      "summary": "Description of the change",
      "affected_scaffold_files": ["internal/web/server.go"],
      "relevant": true,
      "relevance_reason": ""
    }
  ]
}
```

#### Upgrade workflow

1. Run `hamr ai upgrade --json` to get the change report
2. Review and apply relevant changes
3. Run `hamr ai upgrade --applied` to bump the baseline version forward
